Title: Subgenome-specific assembly of vitamin E biosynthesis genes and expression patterns during seed development provide insight into the evolution of oat genome

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Phytyl-diphosphate supply branch for tocopherol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces geranylgeranyl diphosphate to phytyl diphosphate, supplying the phytyl tail used for tocopherol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Included as a vitamin E pathway gene in oat; sequence reconstruction was achieved for partial homeologs, but no strong causal expression–metabolite association was established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Four partial GGR sequences were assembled in oat, suggesting at least homeologs plus possible paralogy. 2. The two largest coding sequences were partial and shorter than wheat/barley orthologs. 3. Because sequences were incomplete, GGR was not used for robust phylogenetic analysis and was not highlighted as correlated with seed vitamin E accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided transcript assembly; SNP inspection; homeolog-specific PCR and Sanger sequencing
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing seeds at 7, 14, 21 and 28 days after anthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential dietary antioxidant for humans; antioxidant function in plants
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; metabolite profiling from previous study for seed tocols; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Four distinct partial sequences were assembled; no full homeolog could be resolved and extra copies may represent paralogs. Lack of full-length overlap prevented phylogenetic analysis and confident inference of pseudogenization.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate to homogentisic acid, the aromatic precursor used for both tocopherol and tocotrienol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point in this study
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of HPPD homeologs is highly positively correlated with accumulation of tocopherols, tocotrienols, and total vitamin E during oat seed development
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three HPPD homeologs were assembled in oat developing seeds. 2. All three HPPD homeologs showed very high positive correlation (r = 0.97-1.0) with tocopherol, tocotrienol, and total vitamin E accumulation. 3. The authors interpret HPPD as one of the key points regulating metabolic flux through the vitamin E pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided transcript assembly; homeolog-specific SNP validation by PCR/Sanger sequencing; developmental expression profiling; correlation analysis with seed vitamin E accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: All three homeologs expressed in developing seeds with similar expression profiles
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing seeds at 7, 14, 21, 28 daa; expression increased in parallel with vitamin E accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential dietary antioxidant for humans; antioxidant protection in plants
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; metabolite profiling from previous study for developing seed tocols; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Only partial coding sequences were assembled, but expression evidence strongly links HPPD to flux into both tocopherol and tocotrienol branches.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for increasing total seed vitamin E by enhancing precursor supply into the pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses homogentisic acid with geranylgeranyl diphosphate to form MGGBQ, the intermediate precursor of tocotrienols
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch-specific intermediate for target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Traditional committed-step enzyme; in this study higher HGGT expression relative to VTE2 is implicated in directing flux toward tocotrienols
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: High HGGT transcript abundance relative to VTE2 is associated with the predominance of tocotrienols over tocopherols in oat seeds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three complete HGGT homeolog coding sequences were assembled. 2. HGGT homeologs were expressed similarly and at relatively constant levels during seed development. 3. HGGT transcript levels were on average ~15-fold higher than VTE2, consistent with the higher abundance of tocotrienols than tocopherols in oat seed and suggesting flux bias toward the tocotrienol branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided assembly; SNP-targeted PCR/Sanger validation; developmental expression profiling compared with tocol accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: All three homeologs expressed in developing seeds at similar levels across development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing seeds at 7, 14, 21, 28 daa; relatively constant expression
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; metabolite profiling from previous study; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Complete coding sequences were obtained for all three homeologs. Authors infer flux direction to tocotrienols is largely driven by transcript abundance ratio HGGT/VTE2 rather than catalytic divergence among homeologs.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to increase tocotrienol-rich vitamin E profiles; likely valuable together with HPPD or VTE4 depending whether total amount or composition is prioritized.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes prenyl-benzoquinol intermediates to form the chromanol ring in vitamin E biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No specific strong expression–vitamin E correlation was highlighted; included as a reconstructed core pathway gene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three partial VTE1 homeologs covering about 90% of ortholog length were assembled. 2. VTE1 was included in phylogenetic and expression analyses of the oat vitamin E pathway. 3. The study did not identify VTE1 as a major determinant of vitamin E accumulation relative to HPPD, VTE2, HGGT, or VTE4.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided assembly; homeolog-specific PCR/Sanger sequencing; developmental expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential dietary antioxidant for humans; antioxidant roles in plants
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Partial but near-complete homeolog coding sequences enabled phylogenetic analysis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses homogentisic acid with phytyl diphosphate to form MPBQ, the committed intermediate for tocopherol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch-specific intermediate for target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Traditional committed-step enzyme; certain homeologs correlated with vitamin E accumulation but overall expression lower than HGGT
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Specific homeologs, especially VTE2_3 and the putative pseudogene VTE2_4, show high positive correlation with tocopherol and total vitamin E accumulation, while overall lower VTE2 expression relative to HGGT is associated with lower tocopherol proportion than tocotrienol in oat seeds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Five VTE2 sequences were assembled in oat; VTE2_4 and VTE2_5 were incomplete and likely pseudogenes due to premature stop codons. 2. VTE2_1 and VTE2_3 transcript abundance was about sevenfold higher than VTE2_2. 3. VTE2_3 and putative pseudogene VTE2_4 showed high positive correlation with tocopherol and total vitamin E accumulation. 4. Average VTE2 transcript abundance was much lower than HGGT, consistent with lower tocopherol than tocotrienol accumulation and suggesting branch flux control.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided assembly; SNP-targeted PCR/Sanger validation; developmental homeolog expression profiling; correlation analysis with tocol accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Homeolog-specific bias in developing seed; VTE2_1 and VTE2_3 higher than VTE2_2 and relatively constant through development
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; metabolite profiling from previous study; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two additional copies likely represent pseudogenes. Expression differences rather than amino-acid divergence were inferred to underlie flux effects.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to shift flux toward tocopherols, especially in combination with managing HGGT expression; care is needed because copy-specific and pseudogene-related complexity exists.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates prenyl-benzoquinone intermediates in the shared vitamin E pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No major direct expression–vitamin E accumulation association was emphasized in this study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three partial VTE3 homeolog coding sequences were assembled. 2. VTE3 was included in phylogenetic and developmental expression analyses. 3. The paper did not identify VTE3 as a principal control point for vitamin E accumulation in oat seed.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided assembly; homeolog-specific PCR/Sanger sequencing; developmental expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Partial homeolog cds lengths were sufficient for expression analysis and mostly for phylogenetic comparison.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal methylation step affecting tocol composition
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates γ and δ forms of tocopherols and tocotrienols to generate α and β forms
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point for vitamin E composition in this study
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: VTE4_1 expression is strongly positively correlated with tocopherol, tocotrienol, and total vitamin E accumulation and is consistent with the α-form-rich vitamin E composition of oat seeds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three complete VTE4 homeolog coding sequences were assembled. 2. VTE4_1 is expressed at the highest level among VTE4 homeologs and shows very strong positive correlation with tocopherol (r = 1.0), tocotrienol (r = 0.99), and total vitamin E (r = 0.99). 3. The other two VTE4 homeologs show negative correlations with vitamin E forms. 4. Because VTE4 converts γ/δ to α/β forms, elevated VTE4_1 expression is consistent with the α-tocotrienol/α-tocopherol-enriched composition of oat seeds. 5. Authors identify VTE4 as a key point regulating vitamin E pathway flux/composition.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Orthology-guided assembly; SNP-targeted PCR/Sanger validation; developmental homeolog expression profiling; correlation analysis with tocol accumulation; interpretation with prior transgenic Arabidopsis evidence cited in discussion
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: VTE4_1 expressed more highly than VTE4_2 and VTE4_3 in developing seed
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Vitamin E composition (especially α-tocotrienol and α-tocopherol forms)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nutritional antioxidant vitamin E; composition affects nutritional quality
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Seed transcriptomics (Illumina RNA-seq; 12 libraries), Roche/454 transcript sequencing; metabolite profiling from previous study; GenBank accessions JZ917187-JZ917210
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Homeolog-specific expression bias was prominent for VTE4. The paper argues that transcript abundance changes, likely due to regulatory elements or epigenetic differences, rather than protein sequence divergence, explain metabolic differences.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target to improve vitamin E composition toward α-forms in oat seeds; especially promising homeolog-specific target (VTE4_1).
